sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A cognitive lab tested whether sleep after study improved foreign-vocabulary retention. Students studied 30 word pairs either in the morning or at night before sleep. Half of each group also completed a short retrieval quiz immediately after study. One-day recall scores were 19 for morning/no quiz, 22 for morning/quiz, 23 for night/no quiz, and 27 for night/quiz. One-week recall scores were 12, 16, 17, and 22, respectively. The night-plus-quiz condition produced the strongest long-term retention.
questions.stem: Which condition showed the highest one-week recall in Figure 1?

choices.B: Night study with an immediate quiz

explanations.B: Correct. The highest one-week score is 22, belonging to the night-plus-quiz condition. Every other group scored lower on the delayed test.
explanations.A: Morning without a quiz had the lowest one-week score, not the highest.
explanations.C: Morning plus quiz improved performance but remained below the night-plus-quiz condition.
explanations.D: Night without a quiz helped, but it still trailed the condition that also used retrieval practice.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. One-day and one-week recall across four vocabulary-learning conditions.

questions.stem: Why might the immediate quiz improve later recall?

choices.A: Retrieval practice strengthens later access to the information
choices.B: It guarantees that every word enters sensory memory permanently
choices.C: It replaces the need for any future review
choices.D: It works only because students become less tired

explanations.A: Correct. Actively retrieving newly learned information can strengthen memory traces and improve later recall. The figure shows this benefit in both morning and night study conditions, supporting a testing effect interpretation.
explanations.B: Sensory memory is brief and does not permanently store learned vocabulary.
explanations.C: Retrieval practice helps but does not eliminate the value of later review.
explanations.D: Reduced tiredness is not the mechanism tested by the immediate quiz.

questions.stem: The advantage of studying at night before sleep is most consistent with what idea?

choices.C: Sleep can support consolidation of recently encoded material
choices.A: Sleep erases interference by eliminating memory entirely
choices.B: Night study always produces the best performance regardless of later testing
choices.D: Consolidation occurs only during conscious rehearsal

explanations.C: Correct. The night-study conditions outperform the matching morning-study conditions at both delays. That pattern is consistent with sleep-related consolidation helping stabilize newly learned associations.
explanations.A: The memory scores show retention, not erasure.
explanations.B: The passage does not justify an absolute claim for every setting or task.
explanations.D: Consolidation is not limited to conscious rehearsal.

questions.stem: A student using the findings would most likely do what?

choices.D: Study important vocabulary before sleep and test themselves soon afterward
choices.A: Avoid self-testing because it causes forgetting
choices.B: Study only in the morning and never review at night
choices.C: Wait a week before the first retrieval attempt

explanations.D: Correct. The strongest condition combined studying before sleep with immediate retrieval practice. Applying the data means combining both of those features when possible.
explanations.A: The figure indicates self-testing improved, rather than harmed, retention.
explanations.B: Morning study was not the strongest pattern shown.
explanations.C: Delaying all retrieval until a week later would skip the benefit of immediate retrieval practice.

questions.stem: Which conclusion is best supported by Figure 1?

choices.C: Both study timing and retrieval practice were associated with better delayed retention
choices.A: Only retrieval practice mattered; sleep timing had no effect
choices.B: Only sleep timing mattered; quizzes were irrelevant
choices.D: The data show that one-week memory is unrelated to the study conditions

explanations.C: Correct. The delayed scores improve both with quizzes and with night-before-sleep study. The strongest performance comes when the two strategies are combined, showing that both variables matter in the observed pattern.
explanations.A: Night study outperformed comparable morning study, so timing did have an effect.
explanations.B: Quizzes improved scores within both study-timing groups, so they were not irrelevant.
explanations.D: The one-week results clearly vary with condition.

questions.passage: A language lab compared monolingual speakers and bilingual speakers on a task-switching test. Participants completed blocks requiring them either to stay with one rule or to switch rules every trial. Response times in the stay condition averaged 620 ms for monolinguals and 600 ms for bilinguals. In the switch condition, the means were 810 ms and 740 ms. Accuracy remained high in all groups, but the bilingual participants showed a smaller switching cost. Researchers suggested that frequent language switching may train cognitive flexibility.
questions.stem: Which group had the smallest switch-related slowdown?

choices.C: Both groups showed identical switch costs

explanations.B: Correct. Bilinguals increased from 600 ms in stay trials to 740 ms in switch trials, a smaller cost than monolinguals, who rose from 620 ms to 810 ms. The passage therefore identifies bilinguals as having the smaller switch cost.
explanations.A: Monolinguals showed the larger increase in response time.
explanations.C: The groups differed in how much they slowed on switch trials.
explanations.D: Switch cost can be inferred by comparing stay and switch times within each group.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Mean response times for stay and switch trials in monolingual and bilingual participants.

questions.stem: Frequent language switching is proposed to benefit which function most directly?

explanations.A: Correct. The lab interprets the smaller switch cost as a sign of greater flexibility in adjusting mental rules. That is an executive-control function closely tied to switching behavior.
explanations.B: Primary socialization concerns learning norms from caregivers and family.
explanations.C: Operant conditioning concerns reinforcement contingencies, not switching cost.
explanations.D: Attachment security is not the construct measured by the task-switching test.

questions.stem: Why is the high accuracy in all groups important for interpreting the response-time differences?

choices.D: It suggests the faster group was not simply trading accuracy for speed
choices.A: It proves bilingualism caused the difference beyond doubt
choices.B: It means the task was too easy to interpret at all
choices.C: It shows response time is irrelevant when accuracy is high

explanations.D: Correct. Because accuracy remained high, the response-time advantage for bilinguals is less likely to be explained by careless responding. That makes the switching-cost comparison more informative.
explanations.A: Observational group comparisons still do not prove causation beyond doubt.
explanations.B: A task can still reveal meaningful response-time differences even with high accuracy.
explanations.C: Response time remains an important performance measure in executive tasks.

questions.stem: A conclusion most consistent with the passage would be which of the following?

choices.B: Bilingual experience may be associated with more efficient switching between mental rules
choices.C: Bilingualism guarantees superiority on all cognitive tasks
choices.D: Language background is irrelevant to executive function

explanations.B: Correct. The data show a smaller switching cost among bilinguals, and the authors cautiously interpret that as possible training in flexibility. The phrasing “may be associated” matches the evidence better than an absolute claim.
explanations.A: The study does not show that monolinguals are incapable of switching.
explanations.C: The task addresses one executive function and cannot justify a guarantee across all cognition.
explanations.D: The results directly suggest that language background may matter here.

questions.stem: What is the main reason the study should avoid claiming that bilingualism alone caused the difference?

choices.A: Participants were compared by existing language background rather than randomly assigned
choices.B: The stay condition was easier than the switch condition
choices.C: Response time was measured in milliseconds
choices.D: Bilinguals and monolinguals were both human

explanations.A: Correct. Because language background is a preexisting characteristic, bilingual and monolingual groups may differ in other ways that influence switching performance. The results are suggestive but observational.
explanations.B: The inherent difficulty of switching does not explain the main causal limitation.
explanations.C: Milliseconds are a normal response-time unit and not the issue.
explanations.D: Being human is not an inferential limitation.
